name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ رَبَّكُمُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُمْ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ (٢١)
```

```english
O mankind! Worship your Lord, who created you and those who were before you, so that you may be Godwary (21)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ فِرَٰشًا وَٱلسَّمَآءَ بِنَآءً وَأَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَخْرَجَ بِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ رِزْقًا لَّكُمْ ۖ فَلَا تَجْعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ أَندَادًا وَأَنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٢٢)
```

```english
—He who made the earth a place of repose for you, and the sky a canopy, and He sends down water from the sky and with it brings forth crops for your sustenance. So do not set up equals to Allah, while you know. (22)
```

```arabic
وَإِن كُنتُمْ فِى رَيْبٍ مِّمَّا نَزَّلْنَا عَلَىٰ عَبْدِنَا فَأْتُوا۟ بِسُورَةٍ مِّن مِّثْلِهِۦ وَٱدْعُوا۟ شُهَدَآءَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٢٣)
```

```english
And if you are in doubt concerning what We have sent down to Our servant, then bring a surah like it, and invoke your helpers besides Allah, if you are truthful. (23)
```

```arabic
فَإِن لَّمْ تَفْعَلُوا۟ وَلَن تَفْعَلُوا۟ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱلنَّارَ ٱلَّتِى وَقُودُهَا ٱلنَّاسُ وَٱلْحِجَارَةُ ۖ أُعِدَّتْ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٢٤)
```

```english
But if you do not—and you will not—then beware the Fire whose fuel will be humans and stones, prepared for the faithless. (24)
```

```arabic
وَبَشِّرِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ أَنَّ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ كُلَّمَا رُزِقُوا۟ مِنْهَا مِن ثَمَرَةٍ رِّزْقًا ۙ قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى رُزِقْنَا مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَأُتُوا۟ بِهِۦ مُتَشَـٰبِهًا ۖ وَلَهُمْ فِيهَآ أَزْوَٰجٌ مُّطَهَّرَةٌ ۖ وَهُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٢٥)
```

```english
And give good news to those who have faith and do righteous deeds, that for them shall be gardens with streams running in them: whenever they are provided with their fruit for nourishment, they will say, ‘This is what we were provided before,’ and they were given something resembling it. There will be chaste mates for them, and they will remain therein [forever]. (25)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** يا أيها الناس اعبدوا (O mankind, worship...) [2:21] etc. After the Glorified has explained the state of the three groups—the God-conscious (المتقين), the disbelievers (الكافرين), and the hypocrites (المنافقين)—and that the God-conscious are upon guidance from their Lord and the Quran is a guidance for them, while the disbelievers have seals upon their hearts and their hearing, and over their vision is a veil, and the hypocrites are diseased and Allah has increased them in disease, being deaf, dumb, and blind (this spanning a total of nineteen verses), He, the Exalted, branched out from that by calling mankind to His worship (العبادة) and to join the ranks of the God-conscious rather than the disbelievers and the hypocrites. This call continues through these five verses until His saying: خالدون (abiding [therein] forever) [2:25]. This context indicates that His saying: لعلكم تتقون (so that you may be God-conscious) [2:21] is connected to His saying: اعبدوا (worship), rather than His saying: خلقكم (created you), even though the meaning is correct under both interpretations.

**And His saying, the Exalted:** فلا تجعلوا لله انداداً وانتم تعلمون (so do not coin any equals for Allah while you know) [2:22]. The word equals (الأنداد) is the plural of *nidd* (ند), which is like *mithl* (مثل)—*a peer or likeness*—in both weight and meaning. The fact that His saying: وانتم تعلمون (while you know) is not restricted by a specific object and is positioned as a circumstantial clause (حال) for His saying: فلا تجعلوا (so do not coin) implies an extreme emphasis in the prohibition. It suggests that a human being, possessing any form of knowledge whatsoever, is not permitted to take equals for Allah, the Glorified, while the reality is that He is the One who created them and those before them, then organized the cosmic system for their sustenance and survival.

**And His saying, the Exalted:** فأتوا بسورة من مثله (then bring a surah like it) [2:23] is a challenge intended to demonstrate incapacity (أمر تعجيزي) in order to manifest the miraculous nature (إعجاز) of the Quran—that it is a Book sent down from Allah in which there is no doubt—a miracle (إعجاز) that remains throughout the passage of ages and the succession of centuries.

This challenge has been repeated in His speech, the Exalted, such as His saying: **قُل لَّئِنِ اجْتَمَعَتِ الْإِنسُ وَالْجِنُّ عَلَىٰ أَن يَأْتُوا بِمِثْلِ هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنِ لَا يَأْتُونَ بِمِثْلِهِ وَلَوْ كَانَ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ ظَهِيرًا** — "Say, ‘Should entire mankind and the jinn muster to produce the like of this Quran, they will not produce the like of it, even if they should back each other up.’" (Al-Isra: 88), and His saying: **أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَرَاهُ قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِّثْلِهِ مُفْتَرَيَاتٍ وَادْعُوا مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتُم مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ** — "Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it?’ Say, ‘Then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated, and invoke whomever you can, besides Allah, should you be truthful.’" (Hud: 13). Based on this, the pronoun in "like it" (مثله) refers back to His saying: مما نزلنا (of what We have sent down), making it a challenge based on the Quran itself and the novelty of its style and expression.

It is also possible that the pronoun refers back to His saying: عبدنا (Our servant), in which case it would be a challenge regarding the Quran from the perspective that the one who brought it was an unlettered man (رجل امي) who never learned from a teacher nor received any of these precious, lofty insights and exquisite, precise expressions from any human being. Thus, the verse would follow the course of His saying: **قُل لَّوْ شَاءَ اللَّهُ مَا تَلَوْتُهُ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَا أَدْرَاكُم بِهِ فَقَدْ لَبِثْتُ فِيكُمْ عُمُرًا مِّن قَبْلِهِ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ** — "Say, ‘Had Allah wished, I would not have recited it to you, nor would He have made it known to you. I have certainly lived out a lifetime among you before it. Do you not exercise your reason?’" (Yunus: 16). Both interpretations have been mentioned together in some narrations (الأخبار).

Know that this verse, like its counterparts, establishes the miraculous nature (إعجاز) of even the shortest surah of the Quran, such as *Surat al-Kawthar* or *Surat al-Asr*, for example. The possibility that the pronoun in "like it" (مثله) refers to the specific surah itself—such as *Surat al-Baqarah* or *Surat al-Yunus*—is rejected by an understanding accustomed to the styles of speech. For whoever accuses the Quran of being a fabrication against Allah, the Exalted, accuses all of it and does not restrict such a claim to one surah over another. Therefore, there would be no meaning in rebutting them by challenging them with *Surat al-Baqarah* or *Surat al-Yunus*, as the meaning would then revert to something like saying: "If you are in doubt regarding *Surat al-Kawthar* or *al-Ikhlas*, then bring a surah like *Surat al-Baqarah*," and the absurdity of this is evident.

## (The Miracle and Its Essence)

**Know:** that the Quran's claim of being a miraculous sign (آية معجزة) through this challenge presented by this verse actually resolves into two claims: the claim of the reality of the miracle (الإعجاز) itself and the breaking of the habitual course of nature (خرق العادة الجارية), and the claim that the Quran is an instance (مصداق) of such miracles. It is well known that the second claim, once established, proves the first. The Quran contents itself with this mode of explanation and challenges by its own self, thereby concluding both results. However, there remains the discussion on how the miracle (الإعجاز) is realized despite its inclusion of things that the habitual course of nature (العادة الجارية) does not affirm—namely, the attribution of effects (المسببات) to their well-known, specific causes (أسباب) without exception in the law of causality (السببية) or failure and variation in the law of causation (العلية). The Quran explains the reality of the matter and removes the doubt therein.

The Quran elaborates on this matter from two perspectives:

**First:** That the miracle (الإعجاز) is a reality, and the Quran is one of its instances, proving the principle of the miracle and its own status as one through the challenge.

**Second:** What is the essence of the miracle (الإعجاز), and how can an event occur in nature that breaks its habit and negates its universality?

## (The Miraculous Nature of the Quran)

There is no doubt that the Quran challenges with its miraculous nature (الإعجاز) in many different verses, both Meccan and Medinan, all of which indicate that the Quran is a miraculous, habit-breaking sign (آية معجزة خارقة). Even the preceding verse—namely His saying: **وَإِن كُنتُمْ فِي رَيْبٍ مِّمَّا نَزَّلْنَا عَلَىٰ عَبْدِنَا فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِّن مِّثْلِهِ** — "And if you are in doubt concerning what We have sent down to Our servant, then bring a surah like it" (2:23), meaning from the like of the Prophet (ص)—is an argument for the Quran being a miracle (معجزة) by challenging them to bring a surah similar to a surah from the like of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم). It is not a direct argument for prophethood (النبوة) without an intermediary. The proof for this is His saying at its beginning: "And if you are in doubt concerning what We have sent down to Our servant," and He did not say, "And if you are in doubt regarding the message (رسالة) of Our servant." Thus, all the challenges occurring in the Quran are arguments for the Quran being a habit-breaking miracle (معجزة خارقة) from Allah. The verses containing the challenge vary in their generality and specificity. Among the most general in challenge is His saying: **قُل لَّئِنِ اجْتَمَعَتِ الْإِنسُ وَالْجِنُّ عَلَىٰ أَن يَأْتُوا بِمِثْلِ هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنِ لَا يَأْتُونَ بِمِثْلِهِ وَلَوْ كَانَ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ ظَهِيرًا** — "Say, ‘Should entire mankind and the jinn muster to produce the like of this Quran, they will not produce the like of it, even if they should back each other up.’" (Al-Isra: 88). This verse is Meccan, and its generality in challenge is beyond doubt for anyone with discernment.

If the challenge were only regarding the eloquence (بلاغة) of the Quran's expression and the grandeur of its style, the challenge would not have extended beyond a specific people—namely, the pure Arabs (العرب العرباء) of the pre-Islamic era and the *Mukhadramun* (those who lived in both the pre-Islamic and Islamic eras) before the language became mixed and corrupted. Yet the verse struck the ears of both mankind and jinn.

Similarly, besides eloquence and grandeur, every specific attribute the Quran contains—such as the true insights (المعارف الحقيقية), virtuous ethics (الأخلاق الفاضلة), legislative rulings (الأحكام التشريعية), reports of the unseen (الأخبار المغيبة), and other knowledges whose veil humanity had not yet lifted at the time of revelation—each of these is something known to some of the two heavy groups (الثقلين) [mankind and jinn] but not all of them. Therefore, the absolute challenge to both groups is only regarding everything in which excellence in attributes is possible.

Thus, the Quran is a sign (آية) for the eloquent in his eloquence and oratory, for the sage (الحكيم) in his wisdom, for the scholar (العالم) in his knowledge, for the sociologist in his sociology, for the legislators in their legislation, for the politicians in their politics, for the rulers in their governance, and for all the worlds in that which they cannot collectively attain, such as the Unseen (الغيب) and the absence of discrepancy in judgment, knowledge, and expression.

From here it becomes clear that the Quran claims the universality of its miraculous nature (إعجاز) from all aspects, in that it is a miracle for every individual of mankind and jinn, whether common or elite, scholar or ignorant, man or woman, a person of卓越 excellence or one of lesser status, provided they possess an intellect that perceives speech. For the human being is created with a primordial nature (الفطرة) to perceive excellence and to recognize increase or decrease therein. Every person can reflect upon the excellence they recognize in themselves or in others and then measure what they have perceived against what the Quran contains, thereby judging with truth and fairness. Is it possible for human power to invent demonstrated divine knowledges that rival what the Quran has brought and equal it in reality? Can it bring ethics built upon the foundation of truths that match what the Quran has brought in purity and virtue? Can it legislate complete jurisprudential rulings (أحكام فقهية) that encompass all human actions without discrepancy leading to contradiction, while preserving the spirit of monotheism (التوحيد) and the word of godwariness (التقوى) in every ruling and its result, and the flow of purity in its root and branch? Can this wondrous encompassment and strange precision originate from an unlettered man (رجل امي) who was raised only in the lap of a people whose share of humanity—despite its countless merits and limitless perfections—was to subsist on raids, battles, and the looting of wealth; to bury daughters alive and kill children for fear of poverty; to boast of ancestors and marry mothers; to pride themselves on immorality and dispraise knowledge while flaunting ignorance? And despite their false pride and zeal, they were humiliated by every oppressor and a prey for every predator—one day for Yemen, one day for Abyssinia, one day for Rome, and one day for Persia. This was the state of the Arabs of the Hijaz in the pre-Islamic era (الجاهلية).

Would any rational person dare to bring a book claiming it to be a guidance for all the worlds, then deposit within it reports of the Unseen (الغيب) from the past and the future, regarding nations that have passed and those yet to come—not just one or two, but in various chapters of stories, epics, and future unseen events—and then not a single one of them deviates from the path of truth?

Can a human being—who is but one part of the realm of material nature (نشأة الطبيعة المادية), where the domain is one of transformation and evolution (التحول والتكامل)—intervene in every affair of the human world and present to the world knowledges, sciences, laws, wisdoms, exhortations, parables, and stories in everything small and large, and then his state does not vary in any of them in terms of perfection or deficiency, even though they were brought into existence gradually and delivered separately, including that which appeared and was then repeated, and branches derived from their roots? This is despite what we see: that no human being remains in one state regarding the perfection or deficiency of their work.

Therefore, the intelligent person capable of conceptualizing these meanings does not doubt that these universal merits and others contained in the Noble Quran are all above human power and beyond material, natural means. And if one is not capable of that, they have not lost their way in their humanity nor forgotten what their primordial conscience (وجدانه الفطري) dictates: to refer to the experts in matters they cannot well test and whose sources they do not know.

If you say: What is the benefit in expanding the challenge to the common folk and exceeding the domain of the elite? For the common folk are quick to be influenced by a call and to respond to any artifice, and they have submitted to the likes of the Bab, the Baha, the Qadiani, and Musaylimah, even though what they brought and argued with was more akin to nonsense and delirium than to speech.

I say: This is the way for the universality of the miracle (الإعجاز) and the possible path for distinguishing perfection and precedence in a matter where competition and excellence occur. For human understandings differ by necessity, as do perfections. The necessary result of these two premises is that the one with high understanding and sound vision perceives, and the one below them in understanding and vision refers to the former. The primordial nature (الفطرة) is the judge, and instinct is the arbiter.

Nothing that a human attains through their perceptive powers and reaches through their understanding can accept universality and encompassment for every individual in every time and place—in terms of access, reach, and survival—except that which is of the nature of knowledge and insight (العلم والمعرفة) according to the mentioned method. For every other supposed miraculous sign (آية معجزة) besides knowledge and insight is a natural entity or a sensory event (حادث حسي) governed by the laws of matter and limited by time and space. Thus, it is not witnessed except by some individuals to the exclusion of others. Even if we assume—as an impossibility or near impossibility—its universality for every individual, it would only be possible in one place rather than all places. And if we assume its expansion to every place, it would not be possible for it to expand to all times and periods.

This, then, is what the Quran challenged with—a universal challenge to every individual in every place and every time.

## (Its Challenge through Knowledge)

It specifically challenged through knowledge and insight (العلم والمعرفة) with His saying, the Exalted: **وَنَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ تِبْيَانًا لِّكُلِّ شَيْءٍ** — "And We have sent down the Book to you as an exposition of all things" (Al-Nahl: 89), and His saying: **وَلَا رَطْبٍ وَلَا يَابِسٍ إِلَّا فِي كِتَابٍ مُّبِينٍ** — "nor anything fresh or withered, but it is in a manifest Book" (Al-An'am: 59), among other verses. For Islam—as known and recognized by anyone who has traversed the core of its teachings—consists of universals provided by the Quran and particulars referred back to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) through such verses as: **مَا آتَاكُمُ الرَّسُولُ فَخُذُوهُ وَمَا نَهَاكُمْ عَنْهُ فَانتَهُوا** — "Take whatever the Apostle gives you, and relinquish whatever he forbids you" (Al-Hashr: 7), and His saying, the Exalted: **لِتَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِمَا أَرَاكَ اللَّهُ** — "that you may judge between people by what Allah has shown you" (Al-Nisa: 105). It addresses both the grand and the subtle of divine "philosophical" knowledges, virtuous ethics, and secondary religious laws—including acts of worship (عبادات), transactions (معاملات), politics, and social affairs—and everything that touches upon human action and work. All of this is based on the primordial nature (الفطرة) and the principle of monotheism (التوحيد), such that the details return to the principle of monotheism through analysis (التحليل), and the principle returns to the details through synthesis (التركيب).

He has explained the permanence of all of them and their applicability to human welfare through the passage and recurrence of ages by His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنَّهُ لَكِتَابٌ عَزِيزٌ لَّا يَأْتِيهِ الْبَاطِلُ مِن بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلَا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ تَنزِيلٌ مِّنْ حَكِيمٍ حَمِيدٍ** — "Indeed it is an august Book: falsehood cannot approach it from before it nor from behind it, [it is] a successive revelation from One All-wise, All-laudable" (Fussilat: 41-42), and His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحَافِظُونَ** — "Indeed We have sent down the Reminder, and indeed We will preserve it" (Al-Hijr: 9). Thus, it is a Book over which the judge of abrogation (النسخ) does not rule, nor does the law of transformation and evolution (التحول والتكامل) pass judgment.

If you say: The views of researchers in sociology and scholars of legislation today have settled on the necessity of the transformation of positive social laws (القوانين الوضعية) according to the transformation of society and their variation with the difference of times and periods and the progress of civilization and culture.

**I say:** The discussion on this matter and the answer to the doubt will come in the commentary (تفسير) of His saying, the Exalted: **كَانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً** — "Mankind were but a single community..." (Al-Baqarah: 213).

The gist of the matter and its summary is that the Quran builds the foundation of legislation upon primordial monotheism (التوحيد الفطري) and instinctive virtuous ethics, claiming that legislation must grow from the seed of creation and existence (التكوين والوجود). These researchers, however, build their view upon the transformation of society while discarding spiritualities—namely the knowledges of monotheism and the virtues of ethics. Thus, their word is frozen upon the course of material social evolution which lacks the virtue of the spirit, while the word of Allah is the highest.

## (The Challenge Regarding the One to Whom the Quran was Revealed)

It has also challenged through the unlettered Prophet (النبي الأمي) who brought the Quran—miraculous in its wording and meaning—without having learned from a teacher or been raised by a mentor, by His saying, the Exalted: **قُل لَّوْ شَاءَ اللَّهُ مَا تَلَوْتُهُ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَا أَدْرَاكُم بِهِ فَقَدْ لَبِثْتُ فِيكُمْ عُمُرًا مِّن قَبْلِهِ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ** — "Say, ‘Had Allah wished, I would not have recited it to you, nor would He have made it known to you. I have certainly lived out a lifetime among you before it. Do you not exercise your reason?’" (Yunus: 16). He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) was among them as one of them, not excelling in any virtue nor speaking with knowledge, to the point that he did not produce anything of poetry or prose for about forty years—which is two-thirds of his life—possessing no precedence nor undertaking any great feat of excellence. Then he brought what he brought suddenly, producing that which their champions were incapable of and before which the tongues of their eloquent ones were exhausted. Then he spread it to the corners of the earth, and no opponent—whether scholar, person of virtue, or one of intellect and cleverness—dared to oppose it.

The most they could take against him was that he traveled to Syria for trade and learned these stories from the monks there. Yet his travels to Syria were only with his uncle Abu Talib before his maturity, and with Maysarah, the servant of Khadijah, when he was twenty-five years old, while being accompanied by those who were with him night and day. Even if we assume that as an impossibility, what are these knowledges and sciences? From where did these wisdoms and truths come? From whom did this eloquence in expression originate, to which necks have bowed and before which eloquent tongues have faltered?

Another thing they took against him was that he used to stand by a blacksmith in Mecca from the people of Rome who used to make swords and sell them. So Allah, the Glorified, sent down: **وَلَقَد نَعْلَمُ أَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ إِنَّمَا يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ لِّسَانُ الَّذِي يُلْحِدُونَ إِلَيْهِ أَعْجَمِيٌّ وَهَٰذَا لِسَانٌ عَرَبِيٌّ مُّبِينٌ** — "We certainly know that they say, ‘It is only a human that teaches him.’ The tongue of him they point to is foreign, while this is a clear Arabic tongue." (Al-Nahl: 103).

They also said about him that he learned some of what he learned from Salman the Persian, who was among the scholars of the Persians, knowledgeable in sects and religions. However, Salman only believed in him in Medina, while most of the Quran was revealed in Mecca, containing all the universal knowledges and stories that were revealed in Medina, or even more. So what did the faith of Salman and his companionship add to him?

Furthermore, whoever reads the Two Testaments and reflects on what is in them, then returns to what the Quran narrates of the histories of the past prophets and their nations, will see that the history is not the same history and the story is not the same story. In them are slips and sins attributed to the righteous prophets of Allah from which the primordial nature (الفطرة) recoils and refuses to attribute even to ordinary righteous and rational people. The Quran exonerates them from these. In them are other matters that do not relate to any true knowledge or ethical virtue, and the Quran mentioned nothing of them except what benefits people in their knowledges and ethics, leaving the rest, which is the majority.

## (The Quran's Challenge through Reporting the Unseen)

It has challenged through reporting the Unseen (الإخبار عن الغيب) in many verses. Among them is its reporting of the stories of the past prophets and their nations, such as His saying, the Exalted: **تِلْكَ مِنْ أَنبَاءِ الْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهَا إِلَيْكَ مَا كُنتَ تَعْلَمُهَا أَنتَ وَلَا قَوْمُكَ مِن قَبْلِ هَٰذَا** — "These are accounts of the Unseen which We reveal to you. Neither you nor your people knew them before this." (Hud: 49), and His saying, the Exalted, after the story of Joseph: **ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ أَنبَاءِ الْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ أَجْمَعُوا أَمْرَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَمْكُرُونَ** — "That is from the accounts of the Unseen which We reveal to you, for you were not with them when they conspired and as they were plotting." (Yusuf: 102), and His saying, the Exalted, in the story of Mary: **ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ أَنبَاءِ الْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ يُلْقُونَ أَقْلَامَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ يَكْفُلُ مَرْيَمَ وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ** — "That is from the accounts of the Unseen which We reveal to you, for you were not with them when they were casting lots [to see] which of them would take charge of Mary, nor were you with them when they were contending." (Al-Imran: 44), and His saying, the Exalted: **ذَٰلِكَ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ قَوْلَ الْحَقِّ الَّذِي فِيهِ يَمْتَرُونَ** — "That is Jesus, son of Mary, a statement of the truth concerning which they are in doubt." (Maryam: 34), among other verses.

Among them is reporting about future events, such as His saying, the Exalted: **غُلِبَتِ الرُّومُ فِي أَدْنَى الْأَرْضِ وَهُم مِّن بعد غَلَبِهِمْ سَيَغْلِبُونَ فِي بِضْعِ سِنِينَ** — "The Romans have been defeated in a nearby territory, but after their defeat they will be victorious in a few years." (Al-Rum: 2-4), and His saying, the Exalted, regarding the Prophet's return to Mecca after the Hijrah: **إِنَّ الَّذِي فَرَضَ عَلَيْكَ الْقُرْآنَ لَرَادُّكَ إِلَىٰ مَعَادٍ** — "Indeed He who has revealed to you the Quran will surely restore you to the destination." (Al-Qasas: 85), and His saying, the Exalted: **لَتَدْخُلُنَّ الْمَسْجِدَ الْحَرَامَ إِن شَاءَ اللَّهُ آمِنِينَ مُحَلِّقِينَ رُءُوسَكُمْ وَمُقَصِّرِينَ لَا تَخَافُونَ** — "You will surely enter the Holy Mosque, if Allah wishes, in safety, with your heads shaven or hair cut short, and without any fear." (Al-Fath: 27), and His saying, the Exalted: **سَيَقُولُ الْمُخَلَّفُونَ إِذَا انطَلَقْتُمْ إِلَىٰ مَغَانِمَ لِتَأْخُذُوهَا ذَرُونَا نَتَّبِعْكُمْ** — "The stay-behinds will say, when you set out to take the spoils, ‘Let us follow you.’" (Al-Fath: 15), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَاللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ** — "and Allah will protect you from the people." (Al-Ma'idah: 70), and His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحَافِظُونَ** — "Indeed We have sent down the Reminder, and indeed We will preserve it" (Al-Hijr: 9), and many other verses regarding the promise to the believers and the threat to the disbelievers and polytheists of Mecca.

From this category are other verses regarding epics (الملاحم), similar to His saying, the Exalted: **وَحَرَامٌ عَلَىٰ قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْنَاهَا أَنَّهُمْ لَا يَرْجِعُونَ حَتَّىٰ إِذَا فُتِحَتْ يَأْجُوجُ وَمَأْجُوجُ وَهُم مِّن كُلِّ حَدَبٍ يَنسِلُونَ وَاقْتَرَبَ الْوَعْدُ الْحَقُّ فَإِذَا هِيَ شَاخِصَةٌ أَبْصَارُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا يَا وَيْلَنَا قَدْ كُنَّا فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ هَٰذَا بَلْ كُنَّا ظَالِمِينَ** — "It is forbidden for [the people of] any town that We have destroyed that they shall return, until when Gog and Magog are let loose and they race down from every slope, and the true promise draws near, behold, the eyes of the faithless will be glazed [as they say], ‘Woe to us! We were certainly oblivious of this. Rather, we were wrongdoers.’" (Al-Anbiya: 95-97), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ** — "Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds that He will surely make them successors in the earth" (Al-Nur: 55), and His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ هُوَ الْقَادِرُ عَلَىٰ أَن يَبْعَثَ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابًا مِّن فَوْقِكُمْ** — "Say, ‘He is able to send upon you a punishment from above you...’" (Al-An'am: 65). Also from this category is His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَرْسَلْنَا الرِّيَاحَ لَوَاقِحَ** — "And We send the fertilizing winds" (Al-Hijr: 22), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَنبَتْنَا فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ شَيْءٍ مَّوْزُونٍ** — "and We caused to grow in it every matter in due balance" (Al-Hijr: 19), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَالْجِبَالَ أَوْتَادًا** — "and the mountains as pegs?" (Al-Naba: 7), the reality of which is based on scientific truths unknown at the time of revelation until the veil was lifted from them by the scientific research that humanity has been granted in these eras.

From this category—and it is among the specialties of this commentary (تفسير) which researches the verses of the Quran by making some speak to others and using some as witnesses for others—is what is in *Surat al-Ma'idah* in His saying, the Exalted: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَن يَرْتَدَّ مِنكُمْ عَن دِينِهِ فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِي اللَّهُ بِقَوْمٍ يُحِبُّهُمْ وَيُحِبُّونَهُ** — "O you who have faith! Should any of you desert his religion, Allah will soon bring a people whom He loves and who love Him..." (Al-Ma'idah: 54), and what is in *Surat al-Yunus* in His saying, the Exalted: **وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَّسُولٌ فَإِذَا جَاءَ رَسُولُهم قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُم بِالْقِسْطِ** — "There is an apostle for every community; so when their apostle comes, judgment is made between them with justice..." (Yunus: 47), and what is in *Surat al-Rum* in His saying, the Exalted: **فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًا فِطْرَتَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْهَا** — "So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith, the nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind..." (Al-Rum: 30), among other verses that inform of the great events that will face the Islamic nation or the world in general after the era of the Quran's revelation. We shall, Allah willing, present a portion of them in the discussion of *Surat al-Isra*.

## The Quran's Challenge through the Absence of Discrepancy within It

It has also challenged through the absence of discrepancy (الاختلاف) within it. The Exalted said: **أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ وَلَوْ كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ غَيْرِ اللَّهِ لَوَجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلَافًا كَثِيرًا** — "Do they not contemplate the Quran? Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would have surely found much discrepancy in it." (Al-Nisa: 82). For it is necessary that the realm is one of material nature (نشأة المادة) and the law governing it is the law of transformation and evolution (التحول والتكامل). There is no existing thing among the entities that are parts of this world except that it is gradual in its existence, moving from weakness to strength and from deficiency to perfection in its essence and all the consequences and appendages of its essence, such as actions and effects. Among these is the human being, who continues to transform and evolve in his existence, actions, and effects, including those effects he reaches through thought and perception. There is not one of us except that he sees himself every day more perfect than yesterday, and he continues to discover in the second moment slips in his actions and errors in his words that originated from him in the first moment. This is a matter no conscious human denies in himself.

Yet this Book was brought by the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) in installments (نجوماً) and he recited it to the people piece by piece over a period of twenty-three years under different circumstances and varying conditions—in Mecca and Medina, by night and day, at home and while traveling, in war and peace, on the day of hardship and the day of victory, the day of safety and the day of fear. It was for the purpose of delivering divine knowledges, teaching virtuous ethics, and legislating religious rulings in all areas of need. Yet there is not found in it the slightest discrepancy in its similar composition—a Book consistent (متشابهاً) and oft-repeated (مثانى). No discrepancy has occurred in the knowledges it delivered and the principles it provided that would contradict one another, nor does any part of it negate another. Thus, the verse explains the verse, and one part clarifies the other, and the whole confirms the whole, as Ali (peace be upon him) said: **ينطق بعضه ببعض ويشهد بعضه على بعض** — "One part of it speaks through another, and one part of it bears witness to another." (*Nahj al-Balaghah*). Had it been from other than Allah, the composition would have differed in beauty and splendor, the speech in eloquence and oratory, and the meaning in terms of corruption and correctness, and in terms of precision and firmness.

If you say: This is merely a claim that does not rely on proof, for contradictions and numerous objections have been taken against the Quran, and perhaps works have been authored regarding them. These are verbal objections returning to its deficiency in aspects of eloquence, and conceptual contradictions returning to its error in its views, perspectives, and teachings. The Muslims have answered them with what in reality returns to nothing but interpretations (التأويلات) that speech following the path of uprightness and the approval of sound primordial nature (الفطرة السليمة) avoids.

I say: The contradictions and objections referred to are present in the books of commentary (تفسير) and elsewhere along with their answers, including this book. Thus, the objection is closer to a claim devoid of explanation.

You will hardly find in these works mentioned by the objector a doubt they raised or a contradiction they took except that it is mentioned in the writings of the commentators along with its answers. So they took the objections, collected them, and arranged them, while leaving the answers and neglecting them. How well it was said: "If the eye of love is suspect, then the eye of hatred is more worthy of suspicion."

If you say: Then what do you say regarding the abrogation (النسخ) occurring in the Quran, which the Quran itself has explicitly stated in His saying: **مَا نَنسَخْ مِنْ آيَةٍ أَوْ نُنسِهَا نَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ مِّنْهَا** — "Any verse that We abrogate or cause to be forgotten, We bring another which is better than it..." (Al-Baqarah: 106), and His saying: **وَإِذَا بَدَّلْنَا آيَةً مَّكَانَ آيَةٍ وَاللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يُنَزِّلُ** — "When We replace a verse with another—and Allah knows best what He sends down..." (Al-Nahl: 101). Is abrogation anything other than a discrepancy in view, even if we grant that it is not of the nature of contradiction in speech?

I say: Just as abrogation is not a contradiction in speech—which is obvious—it is likewise not of the nature of discrepancy in view and judgment. Rather, it arises from the difference in the instance (المصداق) in terms of its acceptance of the application of the ruling one day due to the presence of its benefit (مصلحة) therein, and its lack of acceptance of that application another day due to the change of that benefit to another benefit that necessitates another ruling. Among the clearest witnesses to this is that the verses whose rulings are abrogated in the Quran are accompanied by verbal indicators (قرائن لفظية) hinting that the mentioned ruling in the verse will be abrogated, such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَاللَّاتِي يَأْتِينَ الْفَاحِشَةَ مِن نِّسَائِكُمْ فَاسْتَشْهِدُوا عَلَيْهِنَّ أَرْبَعَةً مِّنكُمْ فَإِن شَهدُوا فَأَمْسِكُوهُنَّ فِي الْبُيُوتِ حَتَّىٰ يَتَوَفَّاهُنَّ الْمَوْتُ أَوْ يَجْعَلَ اللَّهُ لَهُنَّ سَبِيلًا** — "Should any of your women commit an indecency, call four witnesses from among yourselves against them; and if they testify, then confine them to [their] houses until death takes them or Allah ordains a way for them." (Al-Nisa: 15). (Look at the allusion provided by the final phrase). And like His saying, the Exalted: **وَدَّ كَثِيرٌ مِّنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتَابِ لَوْ يَرُدُّونَكُم مِّن بَعْدِ إِيمَانِكُمْ كُفَّارًا** — "Many of the People of the Book desire to turn you back into faithless ones after your faith..." until He said: **فَاعْفُوا وَاصْفَحُوا حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِيَ اللَّهُ بِأَمْرِهِ** — "so excuse and forbear until Allah issues His edict." (Al-Baqarah: 109), where He completed the speech with that which suggests that the ruling is temporary.

## The Challenge through Eloquence

The Quran has challenged through eloquence (البلاغة), as in His saying, the Exalted: **أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَرَاهُ قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِّثْلِهِ مُفْتَرَيَاتٍ وَادْعُوا مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتُم مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ فَإِلَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَكُمْ فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّمَا أُنزِلَ بِعِلْمِ اللَّهِ وَأَن لَّا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ فَهَلْ أَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ** — "Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it?’ Say, ‘Then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated, and invoke whomever you can, besides Allah, should you be truthful.’ But if they do not respond to you, then know that it has been sent down by Allah’s knowledge, and that there is no god except Him. Will you, then, submit?" (Hud: 13-14). This verse is Meccan. And His saying, the Exalted: **أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَرَاهُ قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِّثْلِهِ وَادْعُوا مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتُم مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ بَلْ كَذَّبُوا بِمَا لَمْ يُحِيطُوا بِعِلْمِهِ وَلَمَّا يَأْتِهِمْ تَأْوِيلُهُ** — "Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it?’ Say, ‘Then bring a surah like it, and invoke whomever you can, besides Allah, should you be truthful.’ Rather, they deny that whose knowledge they do not comprehend, and whose interpretation has not yet come to them." (Yunus: 38-39). This verse is also Meccan, and in it is the challenge through composition and eloquence, for that was the manifest affair among the affairs of the Arabs addressed by the verses at that time. History does not doubt that the pure Arabs reached a level of eloquence in speech that history has not recorded for any nation before them or after them, and they trod a path that the feet of others have not trodden in the perfection of expression, the grandeur of composition, the fulfillment of wording, the observance of the occasion, and the ease of speech. The Quran challenged them with every possible challenge that stirs zeal and ignites the fire of pride and tribalism. Their state of vanity regarding their commodity and their arrogance toward submitting to others in their craft is beyond doubt. The period of the challenge lasted long, and the time of the call to action extended, yet they did not respond except with avoidance, and it only increased their incapacity. Their only reaction was hiding and fleeing, as the Exalted said: **أَلَا إِنَّهُمْ يَثْنُونَ صُدُورَهُمْ لِيَسْتَخْفُوا مِنْهُ أَلَا حِينَ يَسْتَغْشُونَ ثِيَابَهُمْ يَعْلَمُ مَا يُسِرُّونَ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ** — "Look! They fold up their breasts to hide from Him. Look! When they cover themselves with their garments, He knows what they keep secret and what they disclose." (Hud: 5).

Fourteen centuries have passed, and no one has brought anything comparable to it, nor has anyone opposed it with anything except that they disgraced themselves and were scandalized in their affair.

Transmission has recorded some of these oppositions and contentions. For instance, Musaylimah opposed *Surat al-Fil* by saying: "The elephant, what is the elephant? And what will make you know what the elephant is? It has a disastrous tail and a long trunk." And in a speech of his regarding revelation addressing Sajah the prophetess: "So we insert it into you an insertion, and we take it out of you an extraction." Look at these deliriums and take heed. And this is a surah with which some Christians opposed the *Fatihah*: "Praise be to the All-Beneficent. Lord of the worlds, the King, the Judge. To You is worship and by You is help sought, guide us to the path of faith," among other such fabrications.

If you say: What is the meaning of a linguistic composition reaching a level that is miraculous for a human being, when the construction of speech is something granted by the human faculty (القريحة)? How can there emanate from the faculty that which it cannot encompass, while the agent is stronger than his act and the source of the effect encompasses his effect? To put it another way: the human is the one who made the word a sign indicating the meaning due to the necessity of social need to make others understand what is in one's mind. Thus, the property of revealing the meaning in the word is a conventional, relative property (خاصة وضعية اعتبارية) established for the human. It is impossible for this property, emanating from the human faculty, to exceed the limit of his faculty and reach a level that the power of the faculty cannot contain. It is impossible, then, for there to be realized in the word a type of revelation that the faculty does not encompass; otherwise, it would be something other than conventional, relative indication (الدلالة الوضعية الإعتبارية). Furthermore, if we assume that among linguistic structures there is a structure reaching the limit of miracle (الإعجاز), it would mean that every intended meaning has various linguistic structures differing in deficiency, perfection, eloquence, and so on, and among those structures is one that is the highest and most eloquent, which human power cannot contain—and that is the miraculous structure. This would necessitate that for every desired meaning, there is only one miraculous structure. Yet the Quran frequently provides different expressions and various structures for a single meaning, which is clear and undeniable in the stories. If its structures were miraculous, there would not be found for each intended meaning except one and no other.

I say: These two doubts and their like are what prompted a group of researchers into the Quran's miraculous eloquence to speak of "diversion" (الصرفة). The meaning of diversion (الصرفة) is that producing the like of the Quran, or ten surahs, or one surah of it, is impossible for humans because of the verses of challenge and the manifestation of incapacity from the enemies of the Quran for centuries—but not because the linguistic compositions within it are in themselves beyond human power and superior to human strength, while all compositions are similar to the type of composition possible for humans. Rather, it is because Allah, the Glorified, diverts (يصرف) the human from opposing it and producing its like by the Divine Will that governs the human will, in order to preserve the sign of prophethood and protect the sanctuary of the message.

This is a false view that does not align with what the verses of challenge indicate by their literal meaning, such as His saying: **قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِّثْلِهِ مُفْتَرَيَاتٍ وَادْعُوا مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتُم مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ فَإِلَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَكُمْ فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّمَا أُنزِلَ بِعِلْمِ اللَّهِ** — "Say, ‘Then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated, and invoke whomever you can, besides Allah, should you be truthful.’ But if they do not respond to you, then know that it has been sent down by Allah’s knowledge..." (Hud: 13-14). The final sentence is manifest in that the argument through the challenge is for the Quran being "sent down" (نازلاً), not speech fabricated by the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), and that its descent is by the knowledge of Allah, not by the descent of devils, as He, the Exalted, said: **أَمْ يَقُولُونَ تَقَوَّلَهُ بَل لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ فَلْيَأْتُوا بِحَدِيثٍ مِّثْلِهِ إِن كَانُوا صَادِقِينَ** — "Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it?’ Rather, they have no faith. Let them bring a discourse like it, if they are truthful." (Al-Tur: 33-34), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا تَنَزَّلَتْ بِهِ الشَّيَاطِينُ وَمَا يَنبَغِي لَهُمْ وَمَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ إِنَّهُمْ عَنِ السَّمْعِ لَمَعْزُولُونَ** — "The devils have not brought it down: it does not behoove them, nor are they capable [of it]. Indeed they are barred from [any] hearing." (Al-Shu'ara: 210-212). The diversion (الصرفة) they speak of would only indicate the truth of the message by the existence of a sign which is the diversion itself, not that the Quran is the speech of Allah sent down from Him. Similar to this verse is the other verse, which is His saying: **قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِّثْلِهِ وَادْعُوا مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتُم مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ بَلْ كَذَّبُوا بِمَا لَمْ يُحِيطُوا بِعِلْمِهِ وَلَمَّا يَأْتِهِمْ تَأْوِيلُهُ** — "Say, ‘Then bring a surah like it, and invoke whomever you can, besides Allah, should you be truthful.’ Rather, they deny that whose knowledge they do not comprehend, and whose interpretation has not yet come to them." (Yunus: 38-39). It is manifest in that what necessitates the impossibility of humans producing the like of the Quran, and the weakness of their powers and the powers of all who help them in undertaking this affair, is that the Quran has an interpretation (تأويلا) whose knowledge they have not encompassed, so they denied it. None encompasses its knowledge except Allah, so He is the One who prevents the opponent from opposing it—not that Allah, the Glorified, diverts them from that while they were capable of it were it not for the diversion by a will from Allah, the Exalted.

Likewise is His saying, the Exalted: **أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ وَلَوْ كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ غَيْرِ اللَّهِ لَوجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلَافًا كَثِيرًا** — "Do they not contemplate the Quran? Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would have surely found much discrepancy in it." (Al-Nisa: 82). It is manifest in that what makes people incapable of producing the like of the Quran is its being in itself characterized by the absence of discrepancy in wording and meaning, and it is not within the capacity of a creature to bring speech devoid of discrepancy—not that Allah diverted them from contradicting it by manifesting the discrepancy within it. Thus, what they mentioned regarding the Quran's miraculous nature being through diversion (الصرفة) is a view that should not be relied upon.

As for the objection that the miraculous nature (الإعجاز) from the perspective of eloquence necessitates the impossible—by the argument that eloquence is among the attributes of conventional speech, and the construction of speech is among the effects of the human faculty (القريحة), so it cannot reach a level of perfection that the power of the faculty cannot contain while it is still an effect of it and not of something else—the answer to it is that what is attributed to the human faculty in speech is only the revelation of the individual word for its meaning. As for the stringing of speech and the arrangement of sentences such that it simulates the beauty of the composed meaning and its form as it is in the mind by its nature—a complete or incomplete simulation, a clear or hidden showing—and likewise the organization of the mental image in the mind such that it corresponds to reality in all its links, premises, concomitants, and consequences, or in many of them, or in some to the exclusion of others—this is a matter that does not return to the convention of words. Rather, it returns to a type of skill in the craft of expression and the art of eloquence granted by the faculty in the stringing of words and the arrangement of linguistic tools, and a type of subtlety in the mind by which the thinking power encompasses the narrated event with its sides, requirements, and attachments.

Thus, there are three aspects here that may coincide in existence or diverge. A person might master a language such that no word escapes his knowledge, yet he is unable to spell or speak it fluently. Another might become skilled in eloquence (البيان) and the flow of speech, yet possess no knowledge of the higher sciences and objectives (المعارف والمطالب); consequently, he fails to speak of them in a manner that preserves the nuances of the meaning or reflects the beauty of the form it holds within itself. A third might delve deeply into a chain of sciences and information, possessing a refined intellect and a delicate primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — yet find himself unable to articulate what is in his mind, or fail to recount the beauty of the meaning and its delightful vista that he witnesses.

These are three matters: the first pertains to man’s convention through his social intellect (القريحة الاجتماعية); the second and third pertain to a type of refinement in the perceptive faculty (القوة المدركة). It is evident that the perception of our perceptive faculties is limited and measured; we cannot encompass the details of external events and factual matters in all their interconnections. Thus, we are never secure from error at any time. Furthermore, the gradual perfection (الاستكمال التدريجي) inherent in our existence necessitates a gradual variation in our knowledge as it moves from deficiency toward completion. Is there any eloquent orator or brilliant poet whose initial works were equal to what his intellect produced in his later years? If we were to assume any human speech—whatever it may be—it would not be safe from error, given the speaker's lack of acquaintance with all parts of reality and its conditions (firstly); nor would it remain at the level of his previous speech or the weight of his subsequent speech. In fact, its beginning would not equal its end, even if we do not perceive it due to the subtlety of the matter, for the law of transformation and perfection is universal (secondly). Accordingly, if we encounter a decisive word (كلام فصل) in which there is no "jest" (الهزل) — and the reality of "jest" is speaking without encompassing knowledge — and which is not subject to inconsistency, then it cannot be human speech. This is what the Quran conveys in His saying: **أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ وَلَوْ كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ غَيْرِ اللَّهِ لَوَجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلَافًا كَثِيرًا** "Do they not contemplate the Quran? Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would surely have found much discrepancy in it" (An-Nisa: 82), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَالسَّمَاءِ ذَاتِ الرَّجْعِ وَالْأَرْضِ ذَاتِ الصَّدْعِ إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلٌ فَصْلٌ وَمَا هُوَ بِالْهَزْلِ** "By the heaven with its returning rain, and by the earth with its splitting [for plants], it is indeed a decisive word, and it is not a jest" (At-Tariq: 11-14). Observe the placement of the oath by the heaven and the earth, both of which are subject to change, and the meaning sworn by in its lack of change and its reliance upon a fixed reality which is its hermeneutic interpretation (التأويل) — *the ultimate reality or objective to which a statement refers* — (and what is intended by the term *ta’wil* in the Quran will be discussed later). And His saying, the Exalted: **بَلْ هُوَ قُرْآنٌ مَّجِيدٌ فِي لَوْحٍ مَّحْفُوظٍ** "Indeed it is a glorious Quran, in a guarded tablet" (Al-Buruj: 21-22), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَالْكِتَابِ الْمُبِينِ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَاهُ قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتَابِ لَدَيْنَا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ** "By the Manifest Book! We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And indeed it is with Us in the Mother Book, surely sublime and wise" (Az-Zukhruf: 2-4). And His saying, the Exalted: **فَلَا أُقْسِمُ بِمَوَاقِعِ النُّجُومِ وَإِنَّهُ لَقَسَمٌ لَّوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عَظِيمٌ إِنَّهُ لَقُرْآنٌ كَرِيمٌ فِي كِتَابٍ مَّكْنُونٍ لَّا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ** "I swear by the places where the stars set! And indeed it is a great oath, if you only knew! It is indeed a noble Quran, in a protected Book—no one touches it except the pure ones" (Al-Waqi'ah: 75-79). These verses and their likes recount the reliance of the Quran in its meanings upon fixed, unchanging realities, and that which relies upon them is likewise unchanging.

Once you recognize what has preceded, you will know that the attribution of the convention of language (وضع اللغة) to man does not necessitate that there cannot exist a linguistic composition beyond what the human who established it is capable of. This is no different than saying that the smith who crafts swords must be the bravest of those who use them, or that the inventor of backgammon and chess must be the most skilled player of them, or that the inventor of the lute must be the most powerful in playing it.

From all this, it has become clear that perfect eloquence (البلاغة التامة) depends on a type of knowledge that corresponds to reality, both in terms of the correspondence of the word to the meaning and the correspondence of the conceptualized meaning to the external world (الخارج) which the mental image (الصورة الذهنية) recounts.

As for the word, it is that the arrangement between the parts of the word according to convention (الوضع) corresponds to the arrangement between the parts of the meaning expressed by the word according to nature (الطبع), such that convention corresponds to nature, as Sheikh Abd al-Qahir al-Jurjani stated in *Dala'il al-I'jaz*.

As for the meaning, its soundness and truthfulness must rely upon the external reality (الخارج الواقع) such that it does not deviate from the truth (الحقيقة) it possesses. This level is that upon which the previous level relies. For how many a "jest" is eloquent in its jesting, yet it cannot withstand "earnestness" (الجد); and how much eloquent speech is built upon ignorance, yet it does not oppose—nor can it oppose—wisdom (الحكمة). The speech that combines the sweetness of the word, the grandeur of the style, the eloquence of the meaning, and the truth of reality is the highest form of speech.

When speech is established upon the foundation of truth, and its meaning perfectly coincides with it, it does not contradict other truths, nor do they contradict it. For the truth (الحق) is harmonious in its parts and unified in its pillars; no truth nullifies another, and no sincerity contradicts another. It is falsehood (الباطل) that conflicts with both falsehood and truth. Consider the significance of His saying, the Glorified and Exalted: **فَمَاذَا بَعْدَ الْحَقِّ إِلَّا الضَّلَالُ** "So what is there after the truth except error?" (Yunus: 32). He has made the truth one, without divergence or scattering. And consider His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ** "and do not follow [other] ways, for they will separate you" (Al-An'am: 153). He has made falsehood scattered and scattering, divergent and dividing.

If this is the case, then no inconsistency occurs between the parts of the truth; rather, there is the ultimate harmony (الإيتلاف), where one part leads to another, and one part produces another, just as one part bears witness to another and recounts another.

This is among the wondrous matters of the Quran, for a verse among its verses is hardly silent in its indication, nor is it barren in its production. Whenever a verse is joined to a suitable verse, it produces a truth from the virgin truths, and then a third verse confirms it and bears witness to it. This is its state and its unique characteristic. You will see throughout the commentaries (البيانات) in this book portions of this, despite the fact that this path has been left untraveled. Had the commentators followed this course, there would have appeared to us by today springs from its sweet seas and treasures from its precious weights.

Thus, the invalidity of the objection from both sides has become clear. The matter of miraculous eloquence (البلاغة المعجزة) does not revolve around the word alone, such that it could be said: "Man is the one who established speech, so how can he not be capable of the most eloquent and fluent speech when he is its establisher?" Nor can it be said: "The most eloquent of conceivable compositions is but one among them, so how can a single meaning be expressed through multiple compositions of differing contexts, all of which exceed human capacity and reach the limit of miracle (الإعجاز)?" Rather, the pivot is the meaning that preserves all aspects of the mind and the external world.

## (Commentary)
### The Meaning of the Miraculous Sign in the Quran and the Explanation of its Reality

There is no doubt regarding the indication of the Quran toward the existence and realization of the miraculous sign (الآية المعجزة) — *the supernatural event that serves as a proof of divine intervention* — in the sense of a matter that breaks the norm (خارق للعادة), indicating the disposal of the supernatural (ما وراء الطبيعة) within the world of nature and the realm of matter; not in the sense of a matter that nullifies the necessity of reason (ضرورة العقل).

The attempts by some who attribute themselves to knowledge to interpret (تأويل) the verses indicating this, in an effort to reconcile them with what appears to be the outward aspects of today's "scientific" natural research, is a rejected affectation.

What the Noble Quran provides regarding the meaning of the "breaker of the norm" and the presentation of its reality, we shall mention in several sections.

## 1 — The Quran’s Affirmation of the Law of General Causality

The Quran establishes causes for natural events and affirms the law of general causality (قانون العلية العامة) just as it is established by the necessity of reason and relied upon by scientific research and discursive views. For man is disposed by nature (مفطور) to believe that every material event has a necessitating cause (علة موجبة) without hesitation or doubt. Likewise, the natural sciences and other scientific inquiries explain events and related matters by what they find of other matters suitable for explanation. By "cause" (العلة), we mean nothing other than the existence of a single matter or a set of matters which, when realized in nature, for example, another matter we call the "effect" (المعلول) is realized by the judgment of experience. This is like the indication of experience that whenever combustion occurs, it must have been preceded by a necessitating cause such as fire, motion, friction, or the like. From here, universality (الكلية) and the lack of failure to occur (عدم التخلف) are among the judgments of cause and effect and their necessary concomitants.

The affirmation of this meaning is apparent in the Quran in its discourse regarding death, life, provision, and other events—whether celestial and heavenly or terrestrial and earthly—in the clearest manner, even though it ultimately attributes them all to Allah, the Glorified, due to the premise of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts*.

Thus, the Quran judges the validity of the law of general causality in the sense that if a cause among causes is realized, along with its necessary conditions of influence and in the absence of any impediment, its effect (مسببه) must necessarily follow by the permission of Allah, the Glorified. And if the effect is found, it inevitably reveals the realization of its cause.

## 2 — The Quran’s Affirmation of that which Breaks the Norm

Furthermore, the Quran narrates and informs of a group of events and occurrences that are not supported by the observed course of the norm (العادة) in the world of nature according to the existing system of cause and effect. These events that break the norm are the miraculous signs (الآيات المعجزة) which it attributes to several of the noble prophets, such as the miracles of Nuh, Hud, Salih, Ibrahim, Lut, Dawud, Sulayman, Musa, ‘Isa, and Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم. These are matters that break the continuous norm in the system of nature.

However, it must be known that these matters and events, though denied by the norm and deemed improbable, are not impossible in themselves (مستحيلة بالذات) such that the necessary reason would nullify them—as it nullifies our saying that affirmation and negation can coexist or be both absent from every aspect, or our saying that a thing can be negated from itself, or our saying that one is not half of two, and similar matters that are impossible in themselves. How could they be so, when the intellects of a vast multitude of religious people since ancient times have accepted and approved of them without denial or rejection? If miracles were impossible in themselves, no rational mind would accept them, they would not be used as evidence for anything, and no one would attribute them to anyone.

Moreover, the essence of these matters—meaning miracles—is not something that the norm of nature denies; rather, they are among the things that the system of matter undergoes at every moment through the transformation of the living into the dead and the dead into the living, the changing of one form into another, one event into another, prosperity into affliction, and affliction into prosperity. The only difference between the work of the norm and the miraculous "breaker of the norm" is that the observed material causes in our hands only produce their effects through specific connections and particular temporal and spatial conditions that necessitate gradual influence. For example, while a staff can potentially become a slithering serpent, and a decayed body can potentially become a living human, this is only realized in the norm through specific causes and particular temporal and spatial conditions by which matter moves from state to state and dons form after form until the final assumed form settles within it, as confirmed by observation and experience—not under any random condition, or without a cause, or by the mere will of a willer, as is apparent from the state of the miracles and supernatural events narrated by the Quran.

Just as simple sense perception and experience do not support the affirmation of these "breakers of the norm," neither does the natural scientific view, because it relies on the observed surface of the system of natural cause and effect—meaning the surface upon which today's scientific experiments and the hypotheses explaining material events are established.

However, the occurrence of events that break the norm, in general, is not within the power of science to deny or conceal. How many a wondrous matter that breaks the norm is performed by the masters of spiritual struggle (أرباب المجاهدة) and the people of asceticism (أهل الإرتياض) every day, filling the eyes, published in journals, and recorded in newspapers and books, such that no doubt remains for a person of intellect regarding their occurrence, nor any uncertainty regarding their realization.

This is what has forced researchers of spiritual phenomena among contemporary scientists to explain it by the flow of unknown waves—electrical or magnetic. They hypothesized that rigorous asceticism grants man authority over the disposal of powerful, mysterious waves that possess or are accompanied by will and consciousness, and thereby he is capable of the wondrous movements, stimulations, and disposals in matter that break the norm through the method of contraction and expansion (القبض والبسط) and the like.

This hypothesis, if completed and applied consistently without contradiction, would lead to the realization of a new, broad hypothesis explaining all the scattered events that were previously explained—wholly or partially—by old hypotheses centered on motion and force, and it would lead all material events to be explained by and linked to a single natural cause.

This is their view, and the truth is with them in general, for there is no meaning to a natural effect that has no natural cause, assuming the natural connection is preserved. In other words, we mean nothing by "natural cause" except the gathering of several natural existents with specific ratios and connections, from which a new, subsequent natural existent is formed, linked to them such that if the system preceding it were disrupted, it would not occur nor would its existence be realized.

As for the Noble Quran, although it does not specify this final natural cause that explains all material events—both normal and those that break the norm (as we perceive it)—by specifying its name and the manner of its influence, as that falls outside its general purpose, it nevertheless establishes for every material event a material cause by the permission of Allah the Exalted. In other words, it establishes for every material event whose existence is attributed to Allah the Glorified (and all are attributed) a material course and a natural path through which the effusion of existence (فيض الوجود) flows from Him, the Exalted, to it. He, the Exalted, said: **وَمَن يَتَّقِ اللَّهَ يَجْعَل لَّهُ مَخْرَجًا وَيَرْزُقْهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَحْتَسِبُ وَمَن يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللَّهِ فَهُوَ حَسْبُهُ إِنَّ اللَّهَ بَالِغُ أَمْرِهِ قَدْ جَعَلَ اللَّهُ لِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدْرًا** "And whoever is wary of Allah, He shall make for him a way out [of adversities], and provide for him from whence he does not reckon. And whoever puts his trust in Allah, He will suffice him. Indeed Allah carries through His command. Certainly Allah has ordained a measure for everything" (At-Talaq: 2-3). The beginning of the verse judges with absolute generality—without restriction—that whoever is wary of Allah and puts his trust in Him, even if the normal causes reckoned by us are causes that dictate the opposite and judge its non-existence, Allah the Glorified is sufficient for him in that, and it will inevitably occur. This is also indicated by the absolute nature of His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذَا سَأَلَكَ عِبَادِي عَنِّي فَإِنِّي قَرِيبٌ أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ الدَّاعِ إِذَا دَعَانِ** "When My servants ask you about Me, [tell them that] I am indeed nearmost. I answer the supplicant’s call when he calls Me" (Al-Baqarah: 186), and His saying, the Exalted: **ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ** "Call Me, I will answer you!" (Ghafir: 60), and His saying, the Exalted: **أَلَيْسَ اللَّهُ بِكَافٍ عَبْدَهُ** "Does not Allah suffice His servant?" (Az-Zumar: 36).

Then the following sentence, which is His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ اللَّهَ بَالِغُ أَمْرِهِ** "Indeed Allah carries through His command" (At-Talaq: 3), explains the absolute nature of the beginning. In this same sense is His saying: **وَاللَّهُ غَالِبٌ عَلَىٰ أَمْرِهِ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ** "Allah is dominant over His affairs, but most people do not know" (Yusuf: 21). This is an absolute sentence, not restricted by anything at all; for Allah the Glorified has a way to every event to which His volition (مشيّته) and will (إرادتة) have attached, even if the normal ways and familiar paths are cut off and non-existent there.

This allows for two possibilities: first, that He, the Exalted, reaches it without a material cause or natural reason, but by the mere will alone; second, that there is a natural cause hidden from our knowledge which Allah the Glorified encompasses, and He carries out what He wills through it. However, the following sentence of the verse, which explains what preceded it—namely His saying, "Certainly Allah has ordained a measure for everything"—indicates the second possibility. For it indicates that every thing among the effects—whether it is what normal causes necessitate or not—has a measure (قدر) that Allah the Glorified has ordained for it, and connections with other existents, and existential links with what is besides it. Allah the Glorified may reach it through these, even if the normal causes are cut off from it and not linked to it. However, these connections and links are not owned by the things themselves such that they obey in one state and disobey in another; rather, they are established by His establishment, the Exalted, being obedient and submissive to Him.

Thus, the verse indicates that He, the Exalted, has established connections and links between all things, through which He may carry out whatever He wills from whichever aspect He chooses. This is not a negation of causality (العلية) and reason (السببية) between things, but an affirmation that they are in the hand of Allah the Glorified, turning them however He wishes and wills. Thus, in existence, there is causality and a real connection between every existent and the existents that precede it in order, except that it is not according to what we find between the outward appearances of existents by habit (and for this reason, we find existing scientific hypotheses falling short of explaining all existential events), but according to what Allah the Exalted knows and organizes.

This reality is what is indicated by the verses of "measure" (القدر), such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِن مِّن شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِندَنَا خَزَائِنُهُ وَمَا نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ** "There is not a thing but its sources are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure" (Al-Hijr: 21), and His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّا كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقْنَاهُ بِقَدَرٍ** "Indeed We have created everything in a measure" (Al-Qamar: 49), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ فَقَدَّرَهُ تَقْدِيرًا** "He created everything and ordained it in a precise measure" (Al-Furqan: 2), and His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّىٰ وَالَّذِي قَدَّرَ فَهَدَىٰ** "who created and proportioned, who determined and guided" (Al-A'la: 2-3). Likewise, His saying, the Exalted: **مَا أَصَابَ مِن مُّصِيبَةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِي أَنفُسِكُمْ إِلَّا فِي كِتَابٍ مِّن قَبْلِ أَن نَّبْرَأَهَا** "No affliction visits the earth or your souls but it is in a Book before We bring it about" (Al-Hadid: 22), and His saying, the Exalted: **مَا أَصَابَ مِن مُّصِيبَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ وَمَن يُؤْمِن بِاللَّهِ يَهْدِ قَلْبَهُ وَاللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ** "No affliction visits [anyone] except by Allah’s leave. Whoever has faith in Allah, He guides his heart, and Allah has knowledge of all things" (At-Taghabun: 11). For the first verse, as well as the rest of the verses, indicates that things descend from the realm of absolute existence (الإطلاق) to the stage of determination (التعين) and individuation (التشخص) by a measure from Him, the Exalted, and a delimitation that precedes the thing and accompanies it. There is no meaning to a thing being limited and measured in its existence except that it is delimited and determined by all its connections with other existents. The material existent is linked to a set of other material existents which are like the mold in which the thing is cast, and which determine its existence, delimit it, and measure it. Thus, there is no material existent except that it is measured and linked to all the material existents that precede it and accompany it; it is inevitably an effect of another like it.

It is also possible to argue for what has preceded using His saying, the Exalted: **ذَٰلِكُمُ اللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ خَالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** "That is Allah, your Lord, the Creator of all things" (Ghafir: 62), and His saying, the Exalted: **مَّا مِن دَابَّةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ آخِذٌ بِنَاصِيَتِهَا إِنَّ رَبِّي عَلَىٰ صِرَاطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ** "There is no moving creature but He holds it by its forelock. Indeed my Lord is on a straight path" (Hud: 56). For these two verses, in conjunction with what was previously mentioned—that the Quranic verses affirm the law of general causality—produce the desired conclusion.

This is because the first verse generalizes creation to everything; there is nothing except that it is a creation of Allah, mighty is His state. The second verse speaks of creation and origination being upon a single course and an organized pattern, without any variation that would lead to chaos or randomness.

The Quran, as you have recognized, affirms the law of general causality among material existents. This results in the fact that the system of existence in material existents—whether they follow the norm or break it—is upon a straight path (صراط مستقيم) that does not fail, and a single course in the attribution of every event within it to the cause that precedes it and necessitates it.

From here, it is concluded that the normal causes, between which and their effects a failure to occur sometimes happens, are not real causes. Rather, there are real causes that are consistent, whose judgments and properties never fail, as may be supported by scientific experiments in the germs of life and in the "breakers of the norm," as previously mentioned.

## 3 — The Quran Attributes what is Attributed to the Material Cause to Allah the Exalted

Furthermore, just as the Quran establishes causality and effectuation between things and affirms the cause-and-effect relationship of some to others, it likewise attributes the matter in its entirety to Allah, the Glorified. From this, it is concluded that existential causes are not independent in their influence, and the real influencer (المؤثر الحقيقي) in the full sense of the word is none but Allah, mighty is His authority. He, the Exalted, said: **أَلَا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَالْأَمْرُ** "Look! All creation and command belong to Him" (Al-A'raf: 54), and He, the Exalted, said: **لِّلَّهِ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ** "To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth" (Al-Baqarah: 284), and He, the Exalted, said: **لَّهُ مُلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** "To Him belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth" (Al-Hadid: 5), and He, the Exalted, said: **قُلْ كُلٌّ مِّنْ عِندِ اللَّهِ** "Say, ‘All is from Allah’" (An-Nisa: 78). This is in addition to many other verses indicating that everything is the pure property of Allah, with no one sharing it with Him. He has the right to dispose of them however He wishes and wills, and no one has the right to dispose of any of them except after Allah grants permission to whom He wills and grants him ownership of the disposal—without independence in this granting of ownership either. Rather, it is a mere permission (إذن) in which the one permitted is not independent, but must rely on the permission of the Permitter. He, the Exalted, said: **قُلِ اللَّهُمَّ مَالِكَ الْمُلْكِ تُؤْتِي الْمُلْكَ مَن تَشَاءُ وَتَنزِعُ الْمُلْكَ مِمَّن تَشَاءُ** "Say, ‘O Allah, Master of all sovereignty! You give sovereignty to whomever You wish, and strip sovereignty of whomever You wish’" (Al-Imran: 26), and He, the Exalted, said: **الَّذِي أَعْطَىٰ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدَىٰ** "He who gave everything its creation and then guided it" (Taha: 50), among other verses. He, the Exalted, also said: **لَّهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ مَن ذَا الَّذِي يَشْفَعُ عِندَهُ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ** "To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth. Who is it that may intercede with Him except with His permission?" (Al-Baqarah: 255), and He, the Exalted, said: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ مَا مِن شَفِيعٍ إِلَّا مِن بَعْدِ إِذْنِهِ** "then He settled on the Throne, directing the command. There is no intercessor, except after His leave" (Yunus: 3).

Thus, causes have come to possess causality through His granting of ownership, and they are non-independent even while they are owners. This meaning is what He, the Glorified, expresses as intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — and permission (الإذن). It is well known that the meaning of "permission" is only sound if there is an impediment to the disposal of the one permitted; and an impediment is only conceivable where there is an existing necessitating factor (مقتض) which the impediment prevents from its influence and stands between it and its disposal.

Thus, it has become clear that in every cause there is an influential principle (مبدأ مؤثر) that necessitates influence, through which it affects its effect, yet the matter nevertheless belongs to Allah, the Glorified.

## 4 — The Quran Establishes an Influence in the Souls of the Prophets regarding Supernatural Events

Furthermore, He, the Exalted, said: **وَمَا كَانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَن يَأْتِيَ بِآيَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ فَإِذَا جَاءَ أَمْرُ اللَّهِ قُضِيَ بِالْحَقِّ وَخَسِرَ هُنَالِكَ الْمُبْطِلُونَ** "No apostle may bring a sign except by Allah’s leave. Moreover, when Allah’s edict comes, judgment is made with justice, and it is then that the maintainers of falsehood are the losers" (Ghafir: 78).

This conveys that the bringing of any sign by any messenger is contingent upon the permission of Allah, the Glorified. Thus, it clarifies that the bringing of miraculous signs by the prophets and their issuance from them is due to an influential principle (مبدأ مؤثر) existing in their noble souls, whose influence is dependent upon permission, as mentioned in the previous section.

He, the Exalted, said: **وَاتَّبَعُوا مَا تَتْلُو الشَّيَاطِينُ عَلَىٰ مُلْكِ سُلَيْمَانَ وَمَا كَفَرَ سُلَيْمَانُ وَلَٰكِنَّ الشَّيَاطِينَ كَفَرُوا يُعَلِّمُونَ النَّاسَ السِّحْرَ وَمَا أُنزِلَ عَلَى الْمَلَكَيْنِ بِبَابِلَ هَارُوتَ وَمَا يُعَلِّمَانِ مِنْ أَحَدٍ حَتَّىٰ يَقُولَا إِنَّمَا نَحْنُ فِتْنَةٌ فَلَا تَكْفُرْ فَيَتَعَلَّمُونَ مِنْهُمَا مَا يُفَرِّقُونَ بِهِ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَزَوْجِهِ وَمَا هُم بِضَارِّينَ بِهِ مِنْ أَحَدٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ** "And they followed what the devils pursued during Solomon’s reign—and Solomon did not faithlessly deny, but it was the devils who faithlessly denied—teaching the people magic and what was sent down to the two angels at Babylon, Harut and Marut, and they would not teach anyone without telling [him], ‘We are only a test, so do not be faithless.’ So they would learn from them that by which they would cause a separation between a man and his wife, though they could not harm anyone with it except by Allah’s leave" (Al-Baqarah: 102). This verse, just as it affirms the validity of magic (السحر) in general, also indicates that magic, like the miracle, is due to a psychic principle (مبدأ نفساني) in the magician, owing to the mention of permission.

In short, all matters that break the norm—whether they are called miracles, magic, or otherwise, such as the charismatic wonders of the saints (كرامات الأولياء) and other traits acquired through spiritual exercises and struggles—are all attributed to psychic principles and volitional necessitating factors, as indicated by His speech, the Glorified. However, His speech explicitly states that the principle existing with the prophets, messengers, and believers is the one that is superior and dominant over every cause in every state. He, the Exalted, said: **وَلَقَدْ سَبَقَتْ كَلِمَتُنَا لِعِبَادِنَا الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِنَّهُمْ لَهُمُ الْمَنصُورُونَ وَإِنَّ جُندَنَا لَهُمُ الْغَالِبُونَ** "Certainly Our decree has gone forth for Our servants, the apostles: it is they who will be the victorious, and it is Our host that will be the triumphant" (As-Saffat: 171-173), and He, the Exalted, said: **كَتَبَ اللَّهُ لَأَغْلِبَنَّ أَنَا وَرُسُلِي** "Allah has ordained: ‘I shall surely prevail, I and My apostles’" (Al-Mujadalah: 21), and He, the Exalted, said: **إِنَّا لَنَنصُرُ رُسُلَنَا وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَيَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْأِشْهَادُ** "Indeed We shall help Our apostles and those who have faith in the life of the world and on the day when the witnesses rise up" (Ghafir: 51). These verses are absolute and unrestricted.

From here, it can be concluded that this existing, victorious principle is a matter beyond nature and above matter.
