For material matters are measured, limited, and overcome by what is above them in measure and limit during conflict and competition. Immaterial (مجردة) matters, although they are also like that, do not involve conflict or mutual impediment unless they attach to matter in some way. This immaterial psychic principle, victorious by the will of Allah the Glorified, when it encounters a material impediment, effuses support upon the cause such that no material cause can resist or prevent it—so understand.

## 5 — The Quran Attributes Supernatural Events to the Command of Allah just as it Attributes them to the Influence of Souls

Furthermore, the final sentence of the verse mentioned in the previous section—namely His saying, the Exalted: **فَإِذَا جَاءَ أَمْرُ اللَّهِ قُضِيَ بِالْحَقِّ** "Moreover, when Allah’s edict comes, judgment is made with justice"—indicates that the influence of this necessitating factor depends upon a command (أمر) from Allah the Exalted that accompanies the permission upon which it also depended. Thus, the influence of this necessitating factor depends upon its coinciding with the command or its unification with it. The "command" has been explained in His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّمَا أَمْرُهُ إِذَا أَرَادَ شَيْئًا أَن يَقُولَ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ** "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be!’ and it is" (Ya-Sin: 82), as the word of origination (الإيجاد) and the saying: "Be" (كن). And He, the Exalted, said: **إِنَّ هَٰذِهِ تَذْكِرَةٌ فَمَن شَاءَ اتَّخَذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِ سَبِيلًا وَمَا تَشَاءُونَ إِلَّا أَن يَشَاءَ اللَّهُ** "This is indeed a reminder. So let anyone who wishes take the way to his Lord. But you do not wish unless Allah wishes" (Al-Insan: 29-30), and He said: **إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَالَمِينَ لِمَن شَاءَ مِنكُمْ أَن يَسْتَقِيمَ وَمَا تَشَاءُونَ إِلَّا أَن يَشَاءَ اللَّهُ رَبُّ الْعَالَمِينَ** "It is just a reminder for all the nations, for those of you who wish to walk straight. But you do not wish unless it is wished by Allah, the Lord of all the worlds" (At-Takwir: 27-29). These verses indicate that the matter which man is able to will and whose reins of choice are in his hand does not become realized as an existent unless Allah wills it—by His willing that man should will, and His intending the intention of man. For these noble verses are in the position of stating that the volitional acts of man, although they are in the hand of man by his will, the will and volition (المشية) are not in the hand of man, but are attributed to the volition of Allah, the Glorified. They are not in the position of stating that everything man wills, Allah has willed it; for that is a grave error, and its consequence would be that the act fails to occur despite the will of Allah when it fails to occur despite the will of man—exalted is Allah above that. Moreover, it contradicts the outward meanings of many verses revealed in this regard, such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَوْ شِئْنَا لَآتَيْنَا كُلَّ نَفْسٍ هُدَاهَا** "Had We wished, We would have given every soul its guidance" (As-Sajdah: 13), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَوْ شَاءَ رَبُّكَ لَآمَنَ مَن فِي الْأَرْضِ كُلُّهُمْ جَمِيعًا** "And had your Lord wished, all those who are on earth would have believed together" (Yunus: 99), among others. Thus, our will and volition, when they are realized in us, are willed by the will and volition of Allah for them; likewise, our acts are willed by Him, the Exalted, through our will and volition as an intermediary. Both of them—meaning the will and the act—depend upon the command of Allah, the Glorified, and the word "Be" (كن).

Thus, all matters—whether they are normal or break the norm, and whether the supernatural event is on the side of good and felicity, such as the miracle and the charismatic wonder, or on the side of evil, such as magic and soothsaying—are attributed in their realization to natural causes. Yet they are nevertheless dependent upon the will of Allah; they do not exist except by the command of Allah, the Glorified—that is, by the cause coinciding with or unifying with the command of Allah, the Glorified.

All things, although they are equal in terms of the attribution of their existence to the divine command—such that if the permission and command are realized, they are realized from their causes, and if the permission and command are not realized, they are not realized (meaning the causality is not completed)—nevertheless, a category of them, which is the miracle from the prophets or what a servant asks of his Lord through supplication (الدعاء), is not devoid of a necessitating will from Him, the Exalted, and a decisive command (أمر عزيمة), as indicated by His saying: **كَتَبَ اللَّهُ لَأَغْلِبَنَّ أَنَا وَرُسُلِي** "Allah has ordained: ‘I shall surely prevail, I and My apostles’" (Al-Mujadalah: 21), and His saying, the Exalted: **أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ الدَّاعِ إِذَا دَعَانِ** "I answer the supplicant’s call when he calls Me" (Al-Baqarah: 186), and other verses mentioned in the previous section.

## 6 — The Quran Attributes the Miracle to an Unconquered Cause

It has become clear from the previous sections of the discussion that the miracle, like other matters that break the norm, does not diverge from normal matters in the need for a natural cause, and that with all of them there are internal causes. The difference between them is that normal matters are inseparable from outward causes that are mostly—or in the majority of cases—accompanied by real natural causes, and with those real causes are the will and command of Allah. Supernatural matters of evil, such as magic and soothsaying, are attributed to natural causes that diverge from the norm, coinciding with the real cause by permission and will. This is like the answering of supplication and the like, without a challenge (تحدي) upon which the manifestation of the truth of the call is based. The miracle is attributed to a real natural cause by the permission and command of Allah when there is a challenge upon which the validity of the prophethood, the messengership, and the call to Allah the Exalted is based. These latter two categories diverge from all other categories in that their cause never becomes overcome or conquered, unlike all other effects.

**If you say:** Based on this, if we were to assume the encompassing and reaching of the natural cause belonging to the miracle, the miracle would be easy and possible to perform for someone other than a prophet as well. No difference would remain between the miracle and other things except in terms of relation and addition only. In that case, a certain matter would be a miracle in relation to one people and not a miracle in relation to others—namely those who are acquainted with its real natural cause—and in one era and not another—namely the era of science. If scientific research were to stumble upon the ultimate real natural causes, no place would remain for the miracle, and the miracle would not reveal the truth. The result of this discussion is that the miracle has no proof-value (حجية) except for the one ignorant of the cause; thus, it is not a proof in itself.

**I say:** Not at all. The miracle is not a miracle because it is attributed to an unknown natural cause, such that it would be stripped of its name when ignorance is removed and lose its proof-value. Nor is it a miracle because of its attribution to a cause that diverges from the norm. Rather, it is a miracle because it is attributed to a matter that diverges from the norm whose cause is never overcome, being an absolutely dominant cause. This is just as the matter occurring by way of the answering of supplication is a charismatic wonder (كرامة) because of its attribution to an unconquered cause—such as the healing of the sick—even though it could occur through other means, such as the method of treatment with medicine. However, in that case, it is a normal matter whose cause can be overcome and conquered by another cause stronger than it.

## 7 — The Quran Considers the Miracle a Demonstration of the Validity of the Messengership, Not a Popular Proof

Here there is a question: What is the connection between the miracle and the truthfulness of the claim of messengership, given that reason does not see a necessary entailment (تلازم) between the truthfulness of the messenger in his call to Allah, the Glorified, and the issuance of a supernatural matter from the messenger? Furthermore, the apparent meaning of the Noble Quran affirms this in what it recounts of the stories of several prophets, such as Hud, Salih, Musa, ‘Isa, and Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم. For they, according to what the Quran narrates, when they spread their call, were asked for a sign indicating the truthfulness of their call, so they answered what they were asked and brought the signs.

Sometimes they were given the miracle at the beginning of the mission before their nations asked them for any of that, as He, the Exalted, said regarding Musa (peace be upon him) and Harun: **اذْهَبْ أَنتَ وَأَخُوكَ بِآيَاتِي وَلَا تَنِيَا فِي ذِكْرِي** "Go, you and your brother, with My signs, and do not flag in My remembrance" (Taha: 42), and He, the Exalted, said regarding ‘Isa (peace be upon him): **وَرَسُولًا إِلَىٰ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ أَنِّي قَدْ جِئْتُكُم بِآيَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ أَنِّي أَخْلُقُ لَكُم مِّنَ الطِّينِ كَهَيْئَةِ الطَّيْرِ فَأَنفُخُ فِيهِ فَيَكُونُ طَيْرًا بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ وَأُبْرِئُ الْأَكْمَهَ وَالْأَبْرَصَ وَأُحْيِي الْمَوْتَىٰ بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ وَأُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا تَأْكُلُونَ وَمَا تَدَّخِرُونَ فِي بُيُوتِكُمْ إِنَّ فِي ذَٰلِكَ لَآيَةً لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ** "and [he will be] an apostle to the Children of Israel, [saying,] ‘I have certainly brought you a sign from your Lord: I will create for you out of clay the form of a bird, then I will breathe into it, and it will become a bird by Allah’s leave. And I heal the blind and the leper and I revive the dead by Allah’s leave. And I will inform you of what you eat and what you store up in your houses. There is indeed a sign in that for you, should you be faithful’" (Al-Imran: 49). Likewise is the giving of the Quran as a miracle to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم. In short, explicit reason does not see a necessary entailment between the truthfulness of what the prophets and messengers brought of the sciences of the Origin and the Return (المبدأ والمعاد) and the issuance of a matter that breaks the norm from them.

In addition, the establishment of radiant demonstrations (البراهين) for these true principles renders the insightful scholar of them in no need of looking into the matter of the miracle. Therefore, it has been said that miracles are for the purpose of convincing the souls of the common folk due to the deficiency of their intellects in perceiving rational truths, whereas the elite are in no need of that.

The answer to this question is that the prophets and messengers (peace be upon them) did not bring miraculous signs to prove any of the sciences of the Origin and the Return that the intellect can grasp, such as monotheism, the resurrection (البعث), and their likes. Rather, they sufficed in that with the proof for the intellect and discourse by way of observation and demonstration, such as His saying, the Exalted: **قَالَتْ رُسُلُهُمْ أَفِي اللَّهِ شَكٌّ فَاطِرِ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** "Their apostles said, ‘Is there any doubt about Allah, the Originator of the heavens and the earth?’" (Ibrahim: 10) in arguing for monotheism, and His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا خَلَقْنَا السَّمَاءَ وَالْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا بَاطِلًا ذَٰلِكَ ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنَ النَّارِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الْمُتَّقِينَ كَالْفُجَّارِ** "We did not create the heaven and the earth and whatever is between them in vain. That is the opinion of those who are faithless. So woe to the faithless on account of the Fire! Shall We treat those who have faith and do righteous deeds like those who cause corruption on the earth? Shall We treat the God-conscious like the vicious?" (Sad: 27-28) in arguing for the resurrection. Rather, the messengers were asked for the miracle, and they brought it, to prove their messengership and verify its claim.

This is because they claimed messengership from Allah through revelation (الوحي), and that it was by a divine speaking or the descent of an angel and the like. This is something that breaks the norm in itself, not being of the genus of outward and inward perceptions that the common people know and find within themselves. Rather, it is a perception hidden from the common souls; if its existence were true, it would be a special disposal from the supernatural within the souls of the prophets only. Yet the prophets are like other individuals among men in their humanity and its faculties. Therefore, they encountered intense denial from the people and violent resistance in rejecting it on one of two grounds:

At times, people attempted to invalidate the claims of the messengers through argumentation, as in His saying, the Exalted: **قَالُوا إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُنَا تُرِيدُونَ أَن تَصُدُّونَا عَمَّا كَانَ يَعْبُدُ آبَاؤُنَا** "They said, ‘You are nothing but humans like us who desire to bar us from what our fathers used to worship’" (Ibrahim: 10). In this, they sought to prove the invalidity of the messengers' claim to divine message (الرسالة) by arguing that they were like all other human beings, and since other people do not find within themselves anything of what the messengers claim for themselves despite their similarity, then if such a thing were possible, it would exist in everyone or be permissible for everyone. Therefore, the messengers responded to their argument as Allah, the Exalted, recounted from them in His saying: **قَالَتْ لَهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ إِن نَّحْنُ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ وَلَٰكِنَّ اللَّهَ يَمُنُّ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَاءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ** "Their apostles said to them, ‘We are indeed nothing but humans like you, but Allah favors whomever He wishes of His servants’" (Ibrahim: 11). Thus, they countered them by conceding the similarity while asserting that the divine message is among the specific favors (المنن) of Allah. Being singled out for certain specific blessings does not contradict being similar in nature; for people have their own unique distinctions. Indeed, if He willed to bestow favor upon whomever He wished among them, He would do so without any hindrance. Thus, prophethood (النبوة) is specific to some, even if it is potentially possible for all.

A similar instance of this argumentation is their saying regarding the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), as recounted by Allah, the Exalted: **أَأُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ الذِّكْرُ مِن بَيْنِنَا** "Has the Reminder been sent down to him out of [all of] us?" (Sad: 8), and their saying as recounted by Allah: **لَوْلَا نُزِّلَ هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنُ عَلَىٰ رَجُلٍ مِّنَ الْقَرْيَتَيْنِ عَظِيمٍ** "Why was not this Quran sent down to some great man from the two cities?" (Az-Zukhruf: 31).

Similar to this argumentation, or close to it, is what is found in His saying, the Exalted: **وَقَالُوا مَالِ هَٰذَا الرَّسُولِ يَأْكُلُ الطَّعَامَ وَيَمْشِي فِي الْأَسْوَاقِ لَوْلَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ فَيَكُونَ مَعَهُ نَذِيرًا أَوْ يُلْقَىٰ إِلَيْهِ كَنزٌ أَوْ تَكُونُ لَهُ جَنَّةٌ يَأْكُلُ مِنْهَا** "And they say, ‘What sort of messenger is this who eats food and walks in the marketplaces? Why has not an angel been sent down to him to be a warner along with him? Or [why is not] a treasure thrown to him, or [why does] he [not] have a garden from which he may eat?’" (Al-Furqan: 7-8). The logic of their deduction was that the claim of divine message necessitates that he should not be a human like us, given that he possesses states of revelation (الوحي) and other things not found in us. So why does he eat food and walk in the marketplaces to earn a living? Rather, an angel should descend with him to share in the warning, or a treasure should be cast to him so he would not need to walk in the markets for gain, or he should have a garden to eat from, rather than eating the same food we eat. Allah, the Exalted, responded to them with His saying: **انظُرْ كَيْفَ ضَرَبُوا لَكَ الْأَمْثَالَ فَضَلُّوا فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَبِيلًا** "Look how they strike similitudes for you! So they have gone astray and cannot find a way" (Al-Furqan: 9), until He said: **وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا قَبْلَكَ مِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِلَّا إِنَّهُمْ لَيَأْكُلُونَ الطَّعَامَ وَيَمْشُونَ فِي الْأَسْوَاقِ وَجَعَلْنَا بَعْضَكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ فِتْنَةً أَتَصْبِرُونَ وَكَانَ رَبُّكَ بَصِيرًا** "We did not send any apostles before you but that they indeed ate food and walked in the marketplaces. We have made some of you a test for others: will you be patient? And your Lord is all-seeing" (Al-Furqan: 20). In another place, the Exalted refuted their demand for the direct presence of an angel for warning by saying: **وَلَوْ جَعَلْنَاهُ مَلَكًا لَّجَعَلْنَاهُ رَجُلًا وَلَلَبَسْنَا عَلَيْهِم مَّا يَلْبِسُونَ** "Had We made him an angel, We would surely have made him a man, and We would surely have made them confused [just] as they are confusing [themselves] now" (Al-An'am: 9).

Close to that argumentation as well is what is found in His saying, the Exalted: **وَقَالَ الَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَاءَنَا لَوْلَا أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا الْمَلَائِكَةُ أَوْ نَرَىٰ رَبَّنَا لَقَدِ اسْتَكْبَرُوا فِي أَنفُسِهِمْ وَعَتَوْا عُتُوًّا كَبِيرًا** "Those who do not expect to encounter Us say, ‘Why are not the angels sent down to us, or [why do] we [not] see our Lord?’ Certainly they are puffed up with pride in themselves and have become terribly defiant" (Al-Furqan: 21). They sought to invalidate the claim of divine message through revelation by demanding that they witness the descent of the angel or see the Lord, the Glorified, based on their similarity to the Prophet. Allah, the Exalted, refuted this by saying: **يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ الْمَلَائِكَةُ لَا بُشْرَىٰ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُجْرِمِينَ وَيَقُولُونَ حِجْرًا مَّحْجُورًا** "The day they see the angels, there will be no good news for the guilty on that day, and they will say, ‘Keep off!’" (Al-Furqan: 22). He mentioned that in their current state, they would not see the angels except at the time of death, as He mentioned in another place in His saying, the Exalted: **وَقَالُوا يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِي نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الذِّكْرُ إِنَّكَ لَمَجْنُونٌ لَّوْ مَا تَأْتِينَا بِالْمَلَائِكَةِ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ مَا نُنَزِّلُ الْمَلَائِكَةَ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَمَا كَانُوا إِذًا مُّنظَرِينَ** "They say, ‘O you to whom the Reminder has been sent down, you are indeed crazy! Why do you not bring us the angels, if you are truthful?’ We do not send down the angels except with the truth, and then they will not be granted any respite" (Al-Hijr: 6-8). These latter verses include an additional aspect of their argumentation, which is the concession of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) truthfulness in his claim, but with the assertion that he is possessed (مجنون) and that what he recounts and reports is merely something suggested to him by madness, not corresponding to reality, as in another place in His saying: **وَقَالُوا مَجْنُونٌ وَازْدُجِرَ** "and they said, ‘[He is] crazy,’ and he was reviled" (Al-Qamar: 9).

In short, such verses are directed toward explaining how they established arguments to invalidate the claim of prophethood through the avenue of similarity (المماثلة).

At other times, they positioned themselves in a state of denial, demanding an argument and a clear proof (البينة) for the truthfulness of the claim, because it included things that souls reject and intellects do not recognize—following the method of "denial with supporting evidence" (المنع مع السند) in the terminology of the art of debate. This clear proof is the miracle (المعجزة) — *the extraordinary act performed by a prophet as a challenge to prove his divine mission*. The explanation for this is that the claim of prophethood and divine message by every prophet and messenger, as recounted by the Quran, was based on the claim of revelation (الوحي) and divine speech (التكليم الإلهي), whether without a medium or through the descent of an angel. This is a matter that is not supported by the senses nor endorsed by empirical experience. Thus, an objection arises from two directions: first, from the lack of evidence for it, and second, from the evidence against it. For revelation, divine speech, and the subsequent legislation (التشريع) and religious upbringing (التربية الدينية) are things that humans do not observe within themselves. The prevailing custom (العادة الجارية) in the system of causes and effects (الأسباب والمسببات) denies it; thus, it is a matter that breaks the norm (خارق للعادة). The law of general causality (قانون العلية العامة) does not permit it. Therefore, if the prophet were truthful in his claim of prophethood and revelation, it would necessitate that he is connected to the metaphysical realm (ما وراء الطبيعة), aided by a divine power capable of breaking the norm, and that Allah, the Glorified, intends by his prophethood and the revelation to him to break the norm. If this were true, and there is no difference between one supernatural act and another, it would be possible for another supernatural act to proceed from the prophet without hindrance, and for Allah to break the norm with another matter that confirms the prophethood and revelation without any impediment. This is because the judgment for similar cases is the same; if Allah intended to guide people through a supernatural way—which is the way of prophethood and revelation—then He should support and confirm it with another supernatural act, which is the miracle.

This is what prompted the nations to ask for a miracle to prove the truthfulness of the claim of prophethood whenever a messenger from among themselves came to them, prompted by primordial nature (الفطرة) and instinct. The request for a miracle was to support the message and confirm it, not to indicate the truthfulness of the right knowledge (المعارف الحقة) to which the prophets invited—such as monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and the Resurrection (المعاد) — *the return of souls to their bodies for judgment* — which can be attained through rational demonstration (البرهان). An analogy for this is if a man were to bring a message to a people from their master who rules over them, carrying commands and prohibitions he claims are from the master. His explanation of these rulings and his establishing proof that these rulings contain the people's welfare—while they know their master only desires their well-being—is sufficient to show that the rulings he brought are true and worthy of being acted upon. However, these proofs and arguments are not sufficient to prove the truthfulness of his message—that their master intended for them, by sending him, the rulings he brought. Rather, they would demand from him a clear proof (بينة) or a sign (علامة) indicating his truthfulness in his claim, such as a letter in the master's handwriting and seal that they can read, or a sign they recognize, as the polytheists said to the Prophet: **حَتَّىٰ تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنَا كِتَابًا نَّقْرَؤُهُ** "until you bring down to us a book that we may read" (Al-Isra: 93).

From what we have mentioned, two things have become clear: First, the necessary correlation (التلازم) between the truthfulness of the claim of divine message and the miracle, and that it is the evidence for the truthfulness of its claim; the elite and the commoners do not differ in its indication and proof. Second, that what the messenger and the prophet find of revelation and perceive from it is not of the same genus as what we find through our senses and our theoretical, discursive intellects (عقولنا النظرية الفكرية). Thus, revelation is not the same as sound thought (الفكر الصائب). This meaning in the Book of Allah, the Exalted, is so clear and radiant that no one with the slightest understanding or the least fairness can doubt it.

A group of researchers from the modern era has deviated in this regard, attempting to build divine knowledge (المعارف الإلهية) and religious truths upon what the natural sciences describe as the primacy of evolving and perfecting matter (أصالة المادة المتحولة المتكاملة). They viewed human perceptions as material properties secreted from the brain, and that existential goals and all real perfections are individual or social material perfections.

They mentioned that prophethood is a type of intellectual genius (نبوغ فكري) and mental clarity through which the person called a "prophet" envisions the social perfection of his people. Through it, he intends to rescue them from the abyss of savagery and barbarism to the arena of civilization and urbanity. Thus, he summons the beliefs and opinions he has inherited and applies them to the requirements of his era and the environment of his life. He then legislates for them social principles and practical universals (كليات عملية) to rectify their vital actions, then completes that with rulings and devotional matters (أمور عبادية) to preserve their spiritual qualities, as a righteous society and a virtuous civilization (المدنية الفاضلة) require such things. From this hypothesis, several points follow:

First: That the prophet is a thinking, genius human being who calls his people to the reform of their social environment.

Second: That revelation (الوحي) is the impression of virtuous thoughts in his mind.

Third: That the Heavenly Book (الكتاب السماوي) is the collection of these virtuous thoughts, purified from psychological whims and personal agendas.

Fourth: That the angels (الملائكة) reported by the prophet are natural forces that manage the affairs of nature, or psychological powers that bestow perfections upon souls; that the Holy Spirit (روح القدس) is a level of the natural material spirit from which these sacred thoughts are secreted; and that the Devil (الشيطان) is a level of the spirit from which bad thoughts are secreted, calling toward wicked acts that corrupt society. In this manner, they interpreted the realities reported by the prophets, such as the Tablet (اللوح), the Pen (القلم), the Throne (العرش), the Chair (الكرسي), the Book (الكتاب), the Reckoning (الحساب), Paradise (الجنة), and Hell (النار), in a way that accords with the aforementioned principles.

Fifth: That religions follow the requirements of their eras and change as they change.

Sixth: That the miracles (المعجزات) narrated from the prophets and attributed to them are fabricated myths or distorted events intended to benefit religion and protect the beliefs of the commoners from changing with the shift of eras, or to protect the positions of religious leaders and heads of the creed from falling and vanishing—among other things that one group innovated and others followed.

This is the gist of what they mentioned. Prophethood in this sense is more worthy of being called a political game than being called divine prophethood. Detailed discussion on the various aspects of what they mentioned is outside the scope of the intended research in this place.

What can be said here is that the Heavenly Books and the narrated prophetic statements in our hands do not agree with this interpretation, nor do they suit it in the slightest. What prompted them toward this type of interpretation was their clinging to the earth and their reliance on material studies; this necessitated the denial of the metaphysical (ما وراء الطبيعة) and the interpretation of realities transcendent above matter in a way that strips them of their status and returns them to inert matter.

What these people mentioned is, in reality, a new development of what others used to mention. The latter used to interpret all the narrated realities in religion as material, except that they affirmed for them existences hidden from the senses—such as the Throne, the Chair, the Tablet, the Pen, the angels, and the like—without the support of the senses or experience for any of that. Then, when the scope of natural sciences expanded and research was conducted on the basis of sense and experience, it became necessary for researchers following that method to deny the material existences of these realities that are outside the senses or far from them, and to interpret them in a way that returns them to a sensible material existence, so that religion would agree with what science has determined and thereby be preserved from falling.

These two groups are between the transgressor and the aggressor. As for the early theologians (المتكلمون), they understood the religious statements according to their intended meanings correctly without metaphor (مجاز), except that they saw all their referents (مصاديق) as purely material things, but hidden from the senses and not governed by the laws of matter at all—whereas reality is otherwise. As for the later researchers of this era, they interpreted the religious statements in a way that removed them from their clear and obvious intended meanings, and applied them to material realities perceived by the senses and confirmed by experience, even though they are not intended, nor do the verbal expressions apply to any of them.

Correct research necessitates that these verbal expressions be interpreted according to what the word provides in convention (العرف) and language (اللغة), then relying for the matter of the referent (المصداق) on how some speech explains others, and then seeing: do scientific views contradict or invalidate them? If something outside of matter and its laws is established therein during this process, the way to it—whether for affirmation or negation—is another mode of research other than the natural research undertaken by the natural sciences. For what does the science researching nature have to do with a matter outside of it? It is not the function of the science researching matter and its properties to address what is other than matter and its properties, neither by affirmation nor negation.

If a researcher among its researchers were to do any of that, it would be an extravagance of speech (شططاً من القول), similar to if a researcher in linguistics wanted to derive from his science a judgment on astronomy, whether in negation or affirmation. Let us return to the rest of the verses.

**And His saying, the Exalted:** "so beware of the Fire whose fuel is humans and stones." The flow of the verses from the beginning of the Surah, although intended to explain the state of the God-conscious (المتقين), the disbelievers (الكافرين), and the hypocrites (المنافقين) — all three groups — yet when He, the Glorified, gathered them all together in His saying: "O mankind! Worship your Lord," and called them to His worship, they necessarily divided into believers and others. For this call, in terms of its acceptance or rejection, does not admit more than two divisions: the believer and the disbeliever. As for the hypocrite, he is only realized by joining the outward to the inward, and the tongue to the heart. Thus, there were those who joined the tongue and the heart in either faith or disbelief, and those whose tongue and heart differed, who is the hypocrite. For what we have mentioned, (perhaps) the hypocrites were omitted from the mention, and it was restricted to the believers and the disbelievers, and faith (الإيمان) was placed in the stead of godwariness (التقوى).

Furthermore, fuel (الوقود) is that with which the fire is kindled, and the verse has explicitly stated that it is the human being himself. Thus, the human is both the fuel and that which is fueled upon, as in His saying, the Exalted, also: **ثُمَّ فِي النَّارِ يُسْجَرُونَ** "then they shall be consumed in the Fire" (Al-Mu'min: 72) and His saying, the Exalted: **نَارُ اللَّهِ الْمُوقَدَةُ الَّتِي تَطَّلِعُ عَلَى الْأَفْئِدَةِ** "It is the fire of Allah, set ablaze, which overspreads the hearts" (Al-Humazah: 6-7). Thus, the human is punished by a fire kindled by his own soul. This sentence is similar to His saying, the Exalted: **كُلَّمَا رُزِقُوا مِنْهَا مِن ثَمَرَةٍ رِّزْقًا قَالُوا هَٰذَا الَّذِي رُزِقْنَا مِن قَبْلُ وَأُتُوا بِهِ مُتَشَابِهًا** "Whenever they are provided with a fruit from it as a provision, they will say, ‘This is what we were provided with before,’ and they were given something resembling it" (Al-Baqarah: 25). It is apparent that the human has nothing there except what he prepared from here, as narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "As you live, so shall you die; and as you die, so shall you be resurrected" — the hadith. Even if there is a difference between the two groups in that the people of Paradise have an "increase" with their Lord. The Exalted said: **لَهُم مَّا يَشَاءُونَ فِيهَا وَلَدَيْنَا مَزِيدٌ** "There they will have whatever they wish, and with Us there is yet more" (Qaf: 35).

What is meant by "stones" in His saying: "whose fuel is humans and stones" are the idols they used to worship. This is evidenced by His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّكُمْ وَمَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ** "Indeed you and what you worship besides Allah are the fuel of hell" (Al-Anbiya: 98), and "hasab" (الحصب) is fuel.

**And His saying, the Exalted:** "they will have in it purified spouses." The context of "spouses" indicates that what is meant by purity (الطهارة) is purity from all types of filth and dislikes that prevent complete harmony, affection, and intimacy—from both physical (خَلقيّة) and moral (خُلقيّة) impurities and dislikes.

## (Narrative Discussion)

Al-Saduq narrated that Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) was asked about the verse, and he said: **الأزواج المطهّرة اللاتي لا يحضن ولا يحدثن** — "The purified spouses are those who do not menstruate and do not defecate."

I say: In some narrations, the purity is generalized to include freedom from all defects and dislikes.
